Back in the 1980's Coca-Cola introduced "New Coke."  It turned into a controversy and everyone was talking about whether or not they liked it.  Many people hated it and demanded that they bring back regular Coca-Cola, which they eventually did.  The theory is that Coca-Cola released an inferior product on purpose.  Everyone wanted to try New Coke and compare it to the original Coca-Cola, so sales increased significantly.  Then sales increased again when they brought back the original, because people wanted to taste it again.  The company denied it, but this theory is compelling because it seems completely plausible.  What large corporation doesn't want to increase sales? I don't have any specific evidence, but I was alive then and I distinctly remember this craze.  Everyone I knew was talking about New Coke and debating about which drink was better.  We were getting together at school and trying it.  I remember it being on TV.  The general consensus among people I knew was that it was nowhere near as good as original Coca-Cola.  It seems like Coca-Cola would have run some focus groups and tested it out before releasing the product if this really was a serious "improvement."

This is very niche but the one conspiracy theory I think might be true is about the sugar lobby in the 1960s, who were angry at President Kennedy for the Cuban embargo, which affected their businesses. Companies like PepsiCo complained publicly about Kennedy and frequently lobbied to try to change the embargo. On the same weekend that Kennedy went to Dallas in November 1963, there was a national soda bottlers' convention being held as well. Pepsi had hired Richard Nixon, who had lost to Kennedy in the presidential run a few years earlier, to appear at the convention, and Nixon slammed Kennedy in the press the day before he was assassinated, not to mention started a false rumor that Kennedy was going to drop Johnson as his VP. Pepsi (not the only company to do this, of course) was trying to turn their business problems into political issues to force Kennedy to change his stance on the embargo. Nixon was reportedly nervous the entire time he was in Dallas, didn't even attend the convention (which was the supposed reason he was there), and when JFK was shot, was visibly shaken and immediately took to telling multiple people that he didn't have anything to do with it, long before anyone even thought that maybe he did. Given Nixon's later manipulation of peace talks between Johnson and North Vietnam, I think it's possible Nixon was either involved or knew a lot more about the assassination than he ever let on. There have been a few articles over the years, some of them focusing more on Joan Crawford who was also there in her capacity as an executive officer of Pepsi at the time. This also comes up in Dorothy Killgallen biographies and conspiracies, it's mentioned in Nixon biographies, and of course the more fringe JFK conspiracy corners of the internet. Mainly I'm interested in the personal observations of those who were around Nixon those two days in Dallas.

Conspiracy theories often involve explanations for events or situations that deviate from the widely accepted narrative. These theories typically suggest secretive, malevolent forces working behind the scenes. It's important to note that while some conspiracy theories may be based on legitimate concerns, many lack credible evidence and can contribute to misinformation.

 one example of a conspiracy theory is the "moon landing hoax" theory, which suggests that the United States faked the Apollo moon landings in the 1960s. Proponents of this theory claim that the moon landings were staged on Earth for various reasons, such as winning the space race against the Soviet Union or distracting the public from other issues.

However, it's important to note that this conspiracy theory has been thoroughly debunked. The Apollo moon landings were extensively documented, and there is overwhelming evidence supporting their authenticity, including photographs, videos, rock samples, and the accounts of thousands of people involved in the missions.

Conspiracy theories often arise due to skepticism, misinformation, or a distrust of official narratives. Debunking such theories involves presenting verifiable evidence and relying on the scientific method to establish the truth.

The JFK conspiracy theory is very compelling, and I think there is enough credible evidence to come to a conclusion that it was a conspiracy. The theory is that the assassination of President John F. My dad was into conspiracy theories when I was a kid, and he told me about the JFK conspiracy. I became obsessed with it and read many books about it. Now that I'm older, it doesn't resonate with me as much, but it does seem like there is enough evidence to conclude that JFK was assassinated through a potential conspiracy. However, it is possible that Lee Harvey Oswald acted alone. Oswald claimed he was a patsy from the start. He had an unusual military career and was likely involved in intelligence work. He was surrounded by CIA assets after his time in the military. Some of the official story is contradictory or confused. The contradictions could be mistakes or an intentional effort to obscure the conspiracy. It never made sense to me that President Kennedy AND Texas Governor both could have shot by Lee Harvey Oswald acting alone with a crappy bolt-action rifle (Mannlicher-Carcano, indelibly etched into my consciousness) from a fourth floor window many yards away from the moving motorcade.  I do not even need the "Magic Bullet Theory" or the "Mysterious Men on the Grassy Knoll"  to make me fully doubt what the Warren Commission was selling.   I don't know what actually did happen, and I do not think we will ever know for sure, but common sense dictates the "lone gunman" story strains credulity. Speculation about the assassination of JFK went on throughout my childhood, teen years and college career.  Lively debates and new research were forever offering fodder contradicting the official explanations offered by the Warren Commission.  Such a seminal event in American history changed the course of all that followed.  I remember hearing many years after the fact that JFK was thinking about pulling the American advisors from Viet Nam after the French abandoned their colonization effort.  "Powers that be"  (whatever the hell that really means)  were unhappy about that and found someone in LBJ who was willing to maintain an American foothold in Southeast Asia no matter the cost.  That is just one theory as to the "why" (one that I like).  The "how" is a whole other ball game.  I think that there are whole libraries one can rummage through filled with speculation in that regard.  Of course, this seminal event of the late twentieth century, will always warrant scrutiny, especially by those (with a sense of curiosity) who were being brought up in its wake.
